quote: So are you a, how you call it, PPA or or you sell the system?
stewart_read: This is Guillermo's first competitive-context signal and an early trigger for verify.prior_designs. He is not just asking about Ion's product — he is revealing he has already been through the PPA-vs-purchase conversation with other companies and is running a filter. Pike correctly defers the product question to the closer, but he does not use this moment to probe competitive context: how many companies has Guillermo seen, what did they offer, what did he like or dislike? That intelligence would sharpen the closer's opening frame.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Pike defers intentionally to avoid scope creep and keep the call moving. This is plausible — but the missed verify.prior_designs depth is a separate gap from scope discipline.
coaching_implication: Show Pike how to use the PPA question as a verify.prior_designs probe: 'Sounds like you've already talked to a few companies — what did you think of what they showed you?' One question, no scope creep, and the closer walks in knowing the competitive landscape.

quote: I'm not interested in the PPA at all. That's that's just like, you can put that in the notes as well.
stewart_read: Guillermo draws a hard line and explicitly asks for it to be noted. Pike does not verbally confirm the note was taken, does not reassure Guillermo that purchase/loan options exist, and moves on. The customer has just handed Pike a trust-building moment — 'I hear you, I've got that down, and I want you to know our specialist works with purchase options' — and Pike lets it pass. This is reactive: there is no evidence Pike is deferring strategically; he simply moves to the next question.
coaching_implication: Drill the verbal confirmation habit: when a customer says 'put that in the notes,' the rep should read it back. 'Got it — no PPA, purchase or loan only. I've noted that.' Costs three seconds, builds trust, and reduces the risk that Guillermo shows up Tuesday expecting a PPA pitch.

quote: the delivery fees are the ones that are, you know, a little bit upsetting.
stewart_read: This is the call's clearest flip opportunity. Guillermo has just articulated genuine bill pain — not the kWh number, but the delivery-fee grievance — and illustrated it with the Ticketmaster analogy at 03:25. Per coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion, the move here is to flip: 'That's exactly what solar addresses — you stop paying Pepco's delivery markup and pay for what you actually generate.' Pike instead says 'of course' and moves to the roof question. The emotional peak of the call is not anchored.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Pike is deferring the emotional anchor to avoid over-promising before the design is built. Possible, but there is no evidence of a later anchor attempt — the bill pain is never returned to.
coaching_implication: Use this moment in the 1-on-1 to introduce the flip mechanic. The Ticketmaster analogy is Guillermo's own language — the rep could have reflected it back: 'That's exactly the problem solar solves — you stop paying Pepco's service fee markup.' No numbers needed. The manager should ask Pike: 'What did you do with the Ticketmaster comment?' If Pike doesn't know, that's the coaching gap.

quote: no matter what you're looking at, that I do know that for sure. There is not a pocket cost or any of these no matter what you look at with us.
stewart_read: At 02:00 Pike explicitly disclaimed knowledge of PPA vs. loan specifics. Here, 90 seconds later, he makes a blanket financing assertion — no out-of-pocket cost on any option — as a reassurance move. This is scope creep: Pike is making a closer-tier claim without closer-tier authority. The claim may be accurate for some options and not others. If the closer walks in and the no-out-of-pocket framing does not apply to the purchase option Guillermo wants, the appointment starts with a trust deficit.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Pike is using a known fact to prevent Guillermo from disengaging over the $0-down concern. Plausible as intent — but the execution contradicts his own stated ignorance and creates a handoff liability.
coaching_implication: This is the scope-creep coaching moment for this call. Show Pike the contradiction: 'At 02:00 you said you didn't know the specifics. At 03:46 you made a blanket financing claim. Which is it?' The correct move is the universal escape hatch: 'What I can tell you is our specialist will walk through all the options — purchase, loan, the works — and make sure you're not out of pocket in a way that doesn't work for you.' Intent preserved, scope held.

quote: What matters most is the, you know, having, like, the panels that I need to cover, like, the the the the consumption for the house.
stewart_read: Guillermo is explicitly stating his purchase criterion: full consumption coverage, not partial offset. This is a high-qualification signal — he is not shopping for a token system, he wants a system sized to his actual load. Pike does not acknowledge or anchor this. The closer needs this as the opening frame for the proposal conversation.
coaching_implication: This line belongs in the handoff text verbatim. The closer's opening should reference it: 'Pike mentioned you want the system sized to cover your full consumption — let me show you what that looks like for your home.' Pike should be coached to recognize consumption-coverage statements as anchor material, not just scheduling noise.

quote: my wife and I, we have eight automated schedules, so it's hard to get us both at the same time, in at the house.
stewart_read: Guillermo's wife is on title (confirmed at 04:23). Here Guillermo explicitly names the scheduling difficulty of getting both of them together. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct move is to anchor the appointment as the venue for both decision-makers and find a time that works for both. Pike does not ask whether the wife will be present on Tuesday at 5PM — he moves directly to confirming the time slot with Guillermo alone. The appointment is booked without the co-decision-maker confirmed.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Pike may assume Guillermo will bring his wife if the time works. But there is no evidence Pike asked, and Guillermo's framing ('hard to get us both') suggests the wife's attendance is not guaranteed.
coaching_implication: This is the protocol violation for this call. Walk Pike through protocols.spouse_decision: the moment Guillermo said 'hard to get us both,' the correct response was 'That's exactly why we want to find a time that works for both of you — this is a decision you'll want to make together. What time works for both of you next week?' The appointment as booked may show with Guillermo alone, which puts the closer in a one-leg close.

quote: I wouldn't be able to. I don't have access to anything like that. So, I can maybe request it, but I'm not I'm I don't know for sure what their what their policy is behind that.
stewart_read: Guillermo asks for the design and cost to be emailed before the appointment. Pike opens with a functional denial ('I wouldn't be able to. I don't have access') — which is a medium-failure hold per the codex's deviation grades — but then hedges with 'I can maybe request it,' which softens the denial without deploying the canonical customer-benefit framing ('our proposals require full explanation otherwise they're confusing'). This maps to 'offered_to_check_with_manager: medium_failure,' not 'caved_and_promised_to_send: critical_failure.' The hold is weak but not a cave.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Pike is using a personal-limitation framing ('I don't have access') rather than a policy framing to avoid confrontation. This is a softer hold that may feel less adversarial to Guillermo. However, the hedge ('I can maybe request it') undermines the hold and leaves Guillermo with an expectation that may not be met.
coaching_implication: Teach Pike the canonical framing from rebuttals.send_proposal_before_meeting: 'Our policy is we don't email proposals beforehand — the reason is they require a full walkthrough to make sense, and our consultant is built to do exactly that.' The customer-benefit framing ('so it makes sense') is more durable than the personal-limitation framing ('I don't have access') because it doesn't invite 'well, can someone else send it?'

quote: Yeah. It'll be my wife too.
stewart_read: Pike asks the title question correctly and captures that the wife is a co-owner. He does not follow through at the appointment-booking stage to confirm she will be present. The verify item is checked; the protocol implication is not acted on. This is the setup for the spouse-handling failure at 08:04.
coaching_implication: The title question and the spouse-confirmation at booking are two steps of the same protocol. Pike executes step one and skips step two. In the 1-on-1, connect these two moments: 'You found out the wife is on title at 04:23. At 08:48 you booked Tuesday at 5PM. Did you confirm she'll be there?' If Pike can't answer, that's the gap.
